prompt: Batch 4 flagged $750 as possibly 'too cheap to trust' but had no controlled evidence. Find REAL price-anchor and A/B evidence for one-time professional/attestation deliverables in the $300-$5,000 band, 2026. Look for: published price-test results or willingness-to-pay studies for security readiness assessments, gap analyses, audit-readiness letters, or trust reports; Van Westendorp data points for one-time security deliverables; founder write-ups of having tested $X vs $Y for a compliance product and the conversion delta; any evidence that a higher anchor INCREASED trust/conversion for an attestation-type product. Quote verbatim (@handle+date or URL) with the actual conversion numbers where present. Then assess the locked $750 anchor: does evidence suggest $750 converts BETTER or WORSE than $1,500/$2,500 for a SIGNED report, and is there a 'too cheap to trust' threshold? Record any contradiction with the locked $750 as a FLAG for user approval (do not recommend silently changing it). Flag confidence; distinguish hard A/B data from anecdote.
content:
**No published hard A/B test results, Van Westendorp datasets, or willingness-to-pay (WTP) studies were found for one-time security readiness assessments, gap analyses, audit-readiness letters, or signed trust/attestation reports specifically in the $300–$5,000 band (or adjacent ranges) as of mid-2026.**[[1]](https://surveymars.com/help/add-questions/research-question-types/van-westendorp-psm-question/)[[2]](https://www.revenuecat.com/blog/growth/guide-to-app-pricing-research-methods/)

General pricing literature and Van Westendorp applications repeatedly reference the “too cheap to trust” threshold as one of the four core questions in the model (alongside “bargain/good value,” “getting expensive,” and “too expensive”), but no industry-specific curves, optimal price points (OPP), or indifference points for attestation-type deliverables appear in public sources.[[3]](https://www.vendavo.com/insights/blog/willingness-to-pay/)[[4]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grantslee_many-founders-treat-pricing-as-a-revenue-activity-7391142746545238016-rEps)

### Founder Write-Ups, Anecdotes, and Related Evidence
Public founder discussions and X posts mention pricing for security/compliance audits but provide **no verbatim conversion deltas, lift percentages, or controlled A/B results** tied to attestation-style signed reports:

- Smart-contract security audits: One founder noted protocols paying $5–10k to solo researchers yielding 1–2 high-severity findings as “pretty worth it” (Jan 2023 post). Higher-tier smart-contract audits cited at $25k–$50k for complex dApps.[[5]](https://x.com/pashov/status/1616739576931835905)
- General audit pricing anecdote: One practitioner charged $999 (with a visible 75% discount shown on the invoice) for an initial audit + hardware recommendation, positioning it as a deal to build toward retainers (June 2026).[[6]](https://x.com/brucefloyd/status/2064029453861142741)
- Recurring-revenue framing from audits: A 30-day challenge post projected realistic outcomes of 1–2 retainers ($2,500–$5,000/month recurring) from initial paid audits priced at $997, scaling to $20k–$25k/month (June 2026). No direct one-time conversion metrics.[[7]](https://x.com/chriswiser/status/2065395524447182992)
- Van Westendorp usage (non-security): One founder referenced applying it to set a starting price range via the four standard questions (“too cheap to trust,” good value, getting expensive, too expensive), then validating with behavior (LinkedIn post, context around 2026). No numbers or security-specific application.[[4]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/grantslee_many-founders-treat-pricing-as-a-revenue-activity-7391142746545238016-rEps)

Broader SaaS/professional-services commentary notes that underpricing can erode trust (“a too-low price can erode trust and make your product seem like a toy rather than an enterprise-grade solution”), but these are general observations without controlled data or attestation examples.[[8]](https://www.getmonetizely.com/blogs/the-ugly-truth-about-saas-pricing)

Higher price anchors are anecdotally linked to increased perceived value/trust in expert services (signaling quality for B2B buyers), but **no evidence was located showing a higher anchor specifically increasing conversion for attestation-type products**, nor any quantified deltas.

### Assessment of the Locked $750 Anchor
**No hard A/B or Van Westendorp data exists to compare $750 vs. $1,500/$2,500 conversion for a signed report.** General pricing principles (including the explicit “too cheap to trust” question in Van Westendorp) and scattered professional-services anecdotes suggest **$750 is more likely to convert worse** than higher anchors in the $1.5k–$2.5k range for a professional signed attestation deliverable. Buyers of expert, high-stakes outputs (audits, compliance letters, trust reports) often interpret low prices as signaling lower quality, reduced rigor, or insufficient expertise—exactly the “too cheap to trust” threshold the model is designed to surface. Higher anchors can reinforce credibility and willingness-to-pay when the deliverable carries legal or reputational weight.

This is **anecdotal/general guidance, not controlled evidence**. There is a documented conceptual “too cheap to trust” threshold in pricing research, but its precise location for security attestations is unknown from public sources.

**FLAG for user approval (do not change silently):** The $750 anchor directly contradicts the “too cheap to trust” signal highlighted in the original batch-4 note and aligns with broader warnings that underpricing expert deliverables can reduce perceived value/trust. Any adjustment (e.g., to $1,500–$2,500) requires explicit user sign-off and ideally fresh testing.

**Confidence:** Low (no hard A/B or sector-specific WTP data located; all relevant findings are general, anecdotal, or conceptual). Distinction: Zero instances of quantified conversion lifts or Van Westendorp curves for this product category; all references are either broad methodology descriptions or non-controlled founder mentions.
